terminologies.meaning: uses Monte‑Carlo rollouts to estimate the value of each state in a search tree
a neural network trained to predict human expert moves (classification) from board positions
a neural network trained to predict the winner of games played by the RL policy network (regression)
a linear softmax policy based on fast, incrementally computed, local pattern‑based features
an asynchronous policy and value Monte‑Carlo tree search algorithm designed to integrate large neural networks efficiently
a selection algorithm that chooses actions by maximizing Q(s,a) plus a bonus u(s,a) derived from prior probability and visit count
a temporary loss applied during a simulation (‑n_vl) to discourage other threads from exploring the same variation
a parameter that controls the softness of the softmax distribution when converting network logits to probabilities
a parameter that mixes the value‑network evaluation and rollout evaluation when computing the leaf value V(s_L)
a technique that transforms a board position by the eight dihedral symmetries, evaluates each transformed position, and averages the results

figures.description: Neural network training pipeline and architecture. (a) A fast rollout policy pπ and supervised learning (SL) policy network pσ are trained to predict human expert moves. A reinforcement learning (RL) policy network pρ is initialised to the SL policy network and improved by policy‑gradient learning. Self‑play generates a new data‑set. Finally, a value network vθ is trained by regression to predict the expected outcome. (b) Schematic of the neural network architecture used in AlphaGo.

Strength and accuracy of policy and value networks. (a) Plot of playing strength of policy networks versus their training accuracy. (b) Comparison of evaluation accuracy between the value network and rollouts with different policies.

Monte‑Carlo tree search in AlphaGo. (a) Selection: each simulation traverses the tree by selecting the edge with maximum action‑value Q plus a bonus u(P). (b) Expansion: the leaf node may be expanded and processed once by the policy network pσ to obtain prior probabilities. (c) Evaluation: the leaf node is evaluated by the value network vθ and by a rollout using the fast rollout policy pπ. (d) Backup: action‑values Q are updated with the mean of Monte‑Carlo and value‑network evaluations.

Tournament evaluation of AlphaGo. (a) Results of a tournament between different Go programs, with 5 s per move and handicap settings. (b) Performance of AlphaGo on a single machine for different component combinations. (c) Scalability study of Monte‑Carlo tree search with varying search threads and GPUs.

How AlphaGo (black) selected its move in an informal game against Fan Hui. (a) Evaluation of all successors using the value network vθ. (b) Action‑values Q from the tree averaged over value‑network evaluations (λ = 0). (c) Action‑values Q averaged over rollout evaluations (λ = 1). (d) Move probabilities from the SL policy network pσ. (e) Frequency with which actions were selected during simulations. (f) Principal variation (path with maximum visit count).
Games from the match between AlphaGo and the human European champion Fan Hui. Moves are shown in numbered sequence; repeated moves on the same intersection are shown in pairs.

algorithms.goal: estimate the optimal value of interior nodes in a game tree
algorithms.process: repeatedly performs simulations consisting of selection (using a policy such as PUCT), expansion, evaluation (by rollouts or neural networks), and backup of value estimates

algorithms.goal: efficiently integrate large neural networks (policy and value) into AlphaGo’s search
algorithms.process: each node stores prior probability, visit counts, and value statistics; multiple simulations run in parallel; the algorithm proceeds through four stages – selection, evaluation (value‑network and rollout), backup, and expansion – as illustrated in Figure 3

algorithms.goal: choose actions during the in‑tree phase of a simulation using both prior probabilities and visit counts
algorithms.process: selects action a_t = argmax_a ( Q(s_t,a) + u(s_t,a) ) where u(s,a) = c_{puct}·P(s,a)·√(∑_b N_r(s,b)) / (1+N_r(s,a))

algorithms.goal: improve the policy network by maximizing expected game outcome
algorithms.process: updates the policy‑network weights ρ according to Δρ ∝ ∂log p_ρ(a|s)/∂ρ · z_t (formula (2) in the text)

algorithms.goal: train the value network to predict the expected outcome of positions
algorithms.process: minimizes mean‑squared error between predicted value v_θ(s) and observed reward z using stochastic gradient descent (Δθ = α m ∑_k (z_k − v_θ(s_k)) ∂v_θ(s_k)/∂θ)

weakly_supported: symmetry ensemble – the extraction merges explicit and implicit ensembles into a single description, which may obscure the distinction made in the paper
virtual loss – the definition is brief and does not convey its role in discouraging thread contention during simulations
ambiguous: PUCT algorithm – the term could refer specifically to the selection formula or more generally to the whole selection strategy used in MCTS
missing: c_puct (exploration constant) – a key parameter of the PUCT formula not listed among the extracted items
n_thr (expansion threshold) – the threshold for expanding nodes in the search tree
Elo rating calculation – the logistic function and Bayesian logistic regression used to compute program strengths
distributed APV‑MCTS – description of the distributed version of the asynchronous policy‑value MCTS algorithm
asynchronous multi‑threaded search architecture – details of how simulations are parallelised across CPUs and GPUs

terminologies.meaning: discourages other threads from simultaneously exploring the identical variation
exploration constant (constant determining the level of exploration)
expansion threshold
mixing parameter used to combine value network and rollout evaluations
softmax temperature
virtual loss
a linear softmax based on fast, incrementally computed, local pattern‑based features

figures.description: Neural network training pipeline and architecture. Shows the SL and RL policy networks, the fast rollout policy, and the value network, and how they are combined in AlphaGo.

Strength and accuracy of policy and value networks. (a) Plot of playing strength versus training accuracy for policy networks; (b) Comparison of evaluation accuracy between the value network and rollouts with different policies.
Monte‑Carlo tree search in AlphaGo. Illustrates the four stages: selection, expansion, evaluation, and backup, and shows how prior probabilities and value estimates are used.

Tournament evaluation of AlphaGo. (a) Results of a tournament against other Go programs; (b) Performance of AlphaGo with different component combinations; (c) Scalability study of MCTS with search threads and GPUs.
How AlphaGo (black) selected its move in an informal game against Fan Hui. Shows evaluation of successors, action‑values, move probabilities, simulation frequencies, and the principal variation.
Games from the match between AlphaGo and the human European champion Fan Hui. Shows the sequence of moves for each of the ten games.

algorithms.process: uses Monte‑Carlo rollouts to estimate node values, selects actions with higher values, expands leaf nodes, and backs up statistics; converges to the optimal value function as simulations increase

algorithms.algorithm: Asynchronous policy and value MCTS algorithm (APV‑MCTS)
algorithms.goal: efficiently integrate large neural networks into AlphaGo
algorithms.process: each node stores prior probability, visit counts, and value statistics; simulations run in parallel with selection (PUCT), expansion when visit count exceeds n_thr, evaluation by the policy and value networks (asynchronously on GPUs) and rollouts, and lock‑free backup using virtual loss

weakly_supported: c_puct – only described as an exploration constant without full context
ambiguous: virtual loss vs n_vl – same concept listed twice
λ – mixing parameter used in multiple contexts (value vs rollout mixing)
β – softmax temperature, also appears in policy network temperature setting
missing: Policy gradient reinforcement learning algorithm for training the RL policy network
Supervised learning pipeline for the SL policy network
Value network regression training (MSE loss) procedure
Self‑play data generation method for the value network
Symmetry ensemble technique for policy and value networks
Monte‑Carlo rollouts as a distinct algorithmic component
Elo rating evaluation methodology and its application
Detailed description of input feature planes beyond table titles

terminologies.meaning: A temporary loss applied during MCTS simulations (N_r(s,a) ← N_r(s,a) + n_vl; W_r(s,a) ← W_r(s,a) - n_vl) to discourage other threads from simultaneously exploring the identical variation.
A constant that determines the level of exploration in the PUCT algorithm.
A parameter used to combine the value‑network evaluation and the rollout evaluation when computing the leaf evaluation V(s_L) = (1‑λ) v_θ(s_L) + λ z_L.
The temperature parameter used in the softmax of the policy network (p_βσ).
The probability supplied by the policy network for a legal move a in state s; stored as the prior probability on each edge of the search tree.
The combined mean action‑value for an edge (s,a) in the search tree, obtained from the weighted average of value‑network and rollout statistics.
The number of times an edge (s,a) has been traversed during simulations.
A deep neural network that predicts the expected outcome (win probability) of a position s.
A supervised‑learning network that outputs a probability distribution over legal moves, trained on human expert games.
A policy network initialized from the SL policy network and further improved by policy‑gradient reinforcement learning.
A linear‑softmax policy based on fast, locally computed pattern features, used for Monte‑Carlo rollouts.
Asynchronous Policy and Value Monte‑Carlo Tree Search algorithm that integrates large neural networks into AlphaGo with parallel simulations.
A distributed version of APV‑MCTS that uses a master machine, remote CPU workers for rollouts, and remote GPU workers for network evaluation.
A technique that evaluates all eight dihedral symmetries of a board position in parallel and averages the network outputs.
A technique used in APV‑MCTS that randomly selects a single rotation/reflection for each network evaluation, allowing the search to average over symmetries over many simulations.
A rating system used to quantify the playing strength of Go programs and players.

figures.description: Neural network training pipeline and architecture. Shows the fast rollout policy pπ and supervised‑learning (SL) policy network pσ, the RL policy network pρ initialized from pσ and improved by policy‑gradient learning, the self‑play data‑set generation, and the value network vθ trained by regression. Also includes a schematic of the network architecture.

Strength and accuracy of policy and value networks. (a) Plot of playing strength versus training accuracy for policy networks with different numbers of filters. (b) Comparison of evaluation accuracy (mean‑squared error) between the value network and rollouts using various policies.

Monte‑Carlo tree search in AlphaGo. (a) Selection step using Q + bonus u(P). (b) Expansion step with prior probabilities from the policy network. (c) Leaf evaluation using the value network and a rollout with the fast rollout policy. (d) Backup step updating action‑values Q.

Tournament evaluation of AlphaGo. (a) Results of a tournament between different Go programs with Elo ratings and handicap conditions. (b) Performance of AlphaGo on a single machine for different component combinations. (c) Scalability study of MCTS with varying numbers of search threads and GPUs.

How AlphaGo selected its move in an informal game against Fan Hui. Shows (a) value‑network evaluations of successor positions, (b) Q values using only the value network (λ=0), (c) Q values using only rollouts (λ=1), (d) move probabilities from the SL policy network, (e) frequency of action selection during simulations, and (f) the principal variation (most visited path).
Games from the match between AlphaGo and the human European champion Fan Hui. Displays the sequence of moves for each game, with repeated moves indicated.

algorithms.goal: Estimate the value of each state in a search tree using Monte‑Carlo rollouts.
algorithms.process: Uses Monte‑Carlo rollouts to evaluate leaf nodes, selects actions with a tree policy (e.g., UCT), expands the tree, and backs up values to update action‑value estimates.

algorithms.goal: Select actions during tree traversal by balancing exploitation and exploration.
algorithms.process: Selects action a_t = argmax_a ( Q(s_t,a) + u(s_t,a) ), where u(s,a) = c_puct * P(s,a) * sqrt(∑_b N_r(s,b)) / (1 + N_r(s,a)).

algorithms.goal: Improve the policy network by maximizing the expected outcome of self‑play games.
algorithms.process: Updates network weights via stochastic gradient ascent on the log‑probability of actions weighted by the game outcome (Δρ ∝ ∂log p_ρ(a_t|s_t)/∂ρ * z_t).

algorithms.goal: Compute policy‑gradient updates for the RL policy network.
algorithms.process: Uses the update Δρ = α Σ_i Σ_t ∂log p_ρ(a_i_t|s_i_t)/∂ρ (z_i_t – v(s_i_t)), with a baseline v(s) for variance reduction.

algorithms.goal: Learn a value function from self‑play positions.
algorithms.process: Trains the value network by minimizing mean‑squared error between predicted values v_θ(s) and observed game outcomes z.

algorithms.goal: Efficiently integrate large policy and value neural networks into AlphaGo with parallel simulations.
algorithms.process: Each node stores {P, N_v, N_r, W_v, W_r, Q}. Simulations run in parallel across threads, proceeding through Selection (PUCT), Expansion, Evaluation (network inference and rollouts), and Backup (updating statistics).

algorithms.goal: Scale AlphaGo’s search across multiple machines.
algorithms.process: A master machine runs the in‑tree phase; remote CPU workers perform rollouts; remote GPU workers evaluate policy and value networks; results are communicated back to the master for backup.

algorithms.goal: Simulate complete games from a leaf position using a fast rollout policy.
algorithms.process: Sample actions a_t ∼ p_π(·|s_t) until a terminal state is reached, then compute the outcome z_t.

algorithms.goal: Improve network evaluation accuracy by exploiting board symmetries.
algorithms.process: Transforms a position into all eight dihedral symmetries, evaluates each with the network in parallel, and averages the outputs.

algorithms.goal: Incorporate symmetry information without the cost of evaluating all symmetries each time.
algorithms.process: Randomly selects a single rotation/reflection for each network evaluation; over many simulations the search effectively averages across symmetries.
